title: Short-time critical dynamics in the classical cubic dimer model
authors: Hu-Xiao Peng, Zheng Yan, Shuai Yin
abstract: The classical dimer model on the cubic lattice hosts a columnar ordered phase and a disordered Coulomb phase, separated by a continuous phase transition that lies beyond the conventional Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson paradigm. While its equilibrium critical properties have been extensively studied, the nonequilibrium critical dynamics of this model--particularly in the short-time regime--remains largely unexplored. In this work, we investigate the short-time critical dynamics near the transition using large-scale Monte Carlo simulations. By quenching the system from both ordered and disordered initial states with vanishing initial correlation length, we analyze the scaling behaviors of the order parameter and its time correlation function in the short-time stage. From these scaling behaviors, we accurately determine the critical temperature $T_c = 0.672(1)$ and the static critical exponent $β/ν= 0.581(5)$ according to the scaling theory of the short-time dynamics. These results are in excellent agreement with previous equilibrium studies. Moreover, we extract the dynamic critical exponent $z = 1.92(1)$ and, notably, find a negative critical initial slip exponent $θ= -1.052(5)$. This unusual negative value contrasts sharply with the positive $θ$ typically observed in conventional critical dynamics. We attribute this anomalous behavior to the combined effects of the emergent SO(5) symmetry at criticality and the local U(1) gauge constraint (Gauss law), which enforces a conserved diffusive dynamics and enhances fluctuations in the short-time regime. Our results provide the first comprehensive characterization of nonequilibrium short-time criticality in the three-dimensional dimer model, shedding new light on the universal dynamical features of phase transitions beyond the Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson framework.
summary: This work characterizes the non-equilibrium short-time critical dynamics of the 3D classical dimer model. Using Monte Carlo simulations, the authors determine key critical exponents, notably finding an unusual negative critical initial slip exponent. This negative exponent is linked theoretically to the combined effects of emergent SO(5) symmetry and a local U(1) gauge constraint.
main_problem: Investigating the nonequilibrium critical dynamics, particularly in the short-time regime, of the classical cubic dimer model.
main_result: The authors determined the critical temperature ($T_c = 0.672(1)$), dynamic exponent ($z = 1.92(1)$), and found an anomalous negative critical initial slip exponent ($	heta = -1.052(5)$).
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations were used to analyze the short-time scaling behavior of the order parameter and its time correlation function.
model_or_system: The system is the classical dimer model on a cubic lattice, which exhibits a continuous phase transition beyond the conventional Landau-Ginzburg-Wilson paradigm.
key_observables:
Order parameter squared ($\langle N^2 angle$), time-correlation function ($Q(t)$), critical exponents ($ eta/
u$, $z$, $	heta$).
important_parameters: The study focuses on the short-time regime, quenching from both ordered and disordered initial states.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on theoretical scaling relations for short-time dynamics, and the negative $	heta$ is attributed to emergent SO(5) symmetry and a local U(1) gauge constraint.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of the order parameter on time and system size, showing data collapse when rescaled using the derived exponents.
paper_structure: The paper details the model and methods, reviews the theory of short-time scaling, presents the numerical results for the exponents, and concludes by interpreting the physical meaning of the anomalous negative exponent $	heta$.
why_interesting: The study provides a detailed look at non-equilibrium critical dynamics, which shares conceptual links with how quantum systems evolve away from equilibrium, particularly in understanding universal scaling laws beyond simple mean-field descriptions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two types of interactions and two types of initial states in the dimer model. (a) Two parallel dimers on the plane contribute energy v2. (b) Four parallel dimers on a unit cube contribute energy v4. In this study, we fixed v2 = −1 and v4 = 10. (c) The disordered initial state with Nx = 0, typical of a Coulomb phase. (d) The completely ordered initial state with Nx = 1, representing a columnar phase.

FIG. 3. Short-time dynamics of the ratio N 2 d/N 2 o at the crit- ical point Tc = 0.672 for system size L = 100. The dotted curve represents the Monte Carlo results, while the solid line corresponds to the fitted curve with a power function. Log- log coordinates are used.

FIG. 4. Short-time dynamics of the classical dimer model quenched from a disordered state to the critical point for various system sizes. (a) N 2 d plotted versus time in log-log scale for system size L = 100 . The symbols represent the Monte Carlo results, while the solid line corresponds to the fitted result. The inset shows a log-log plot of N 2 d as a function of system size L = 40, 60, 80, 100 at a fixed time t = 104, where the solid line represents a power-law fit yielding N 2 d ∝L−3.12, consistent with the expected L−d scaling with d = 3. (b) Time dependence of N 2 d for L = 40 to 100. (c) After rescaling N 2 d and t as N 2 dL2β/νz

FIG. 5. Short-time dynamics of the classical dimer model quenched from an ordered state to the critical point for various system sizes. (a) N 2 o plotted versus time in log-log scale for system size L = 100 . (b) Time dependence of N 2 o for L = 40 to 100. (c) After rescaling N 2 o and t as N 2 o L2β/νz and tL−z, all curves collapse well. Log-log coordinates are used.

FIG. 6. The time correlation of the total order parameter Q starting from a disordered state at the critical point. (a) Time dependence of Q for system size L = 80. Since the statistical fluctuations for L = 100 are too large to allow for a reliable fit, we therefore use L = 80 in our analysis. (b) After rescaling Q and t as QL−θz and tL−z, all curves collapse well. Log-log coordinates are used.
